lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

It is only in reference to a particular point in his dispute with Candidus that Origen alleges this falsification. The story of Hilary's being condemned through his writings having been falsified has no foundation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Now compare the words of Origen, which I have translated word for word above, with these which by him have been turned into Latin, or rather overturned; and you will see clearly how great a discrepancy between them there is, not only of word but of meaning.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I beg you not to consider my translation wearisome because it is longer; for the object I had in translating the whole passage was to exhibit the purpose which he had in suppressing the earlier part.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: There exists in Greek a dialogue between Origen and Candidus the defender of the heresy of Valentinian, in which I confess it seems to me when I read it that I am looking on at a fight between two Andabatian gladiators.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Candidus maintains that the Son is of the substance of the Father, falling into the error of asserting a Probolé or Production.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: On the other side, Origen, like Arius and Eunomius, refuses to admit that He is produced or born, lest God the Father should thus be divided into parts; but he says that He was a sublime and most excellent creation who came into being by the will of the Father like other creatures.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: They then come to a second question.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Candidus asserts that the devil is of a nature wholly evil which can never be saved.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Against this Origen rightly asserts that he is not of perishable substance, but that it is by his own will that he fell and can be saved.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This Candidus falsely turns into a reproach against Origen, as if he had said that the diabolical nature could be saved.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What therefore Candidus had falsely accused him of, Origen refutes.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But we see that in this Dialogue alone Origen accuses the heretics of having falsified his writings, not in the other books about which no question was ever raised.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Otherwise, if we are to believe that all which is heretical is not due to Origen but to the heretics, while almost all his books are full of these errors, nothing of Origen’s will remain, but everything must be the work of those of whose names we are ignorant.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is not enough for him to calumniate the Greeks and the men of old time, about whom the distance either of time or space gives him the power to tell any falsehood he pleases.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He comes to the Latins, and first takes the case of Hilary the Confessor, whose book, he states, was falsified by the heretics after the Council of Ariminum.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: A question arose about him on this account in a council of bishops, and he then ordered the book to be brought from his own house.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The book in its heretical shape was in his desk, though he did not know it; and when it was produced, the author of the book was condemned as a heretic and excommunicated, and left the council room.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This is the story, a mere dream of his own, which he tells to his intimates; and he imagines his authority to be so great that no one will dare to contradict him when he says such things.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will ask him a few questions.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In what city was the synod held by which Hilary was excommunicated?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What were the names of the Bishops present?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who subscribed the sentence?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who were content, and who non-content?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who were the consuls of the year? and who was the emperor who ordered the assembly of the council?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Were the Bishops present those of Gaul alone, or of Italy and Spain as well? and for what purpose was the council called together?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You tell us none of these things; yet, in order to defend Origen, you treat as a criminal and as excommunicated a man of the highest eloquence, the very clarion of the Latin tongue against the Arians.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But we are in the presence of a confessor, and even his calumnies must be borne with patience.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He next passes to Cyprian the illustrious martyr, and he tells us that a book by Tertullian entitled “On the Trinity” is read as one of his works by the partisans of the Macedonian heresy at Constantinople.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In this charge of his he tells two falsehoods.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The book in question is not Tertullian’s, nor does it pass under the name of Cyprian.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is by Novatian and is called by his name; the peculiarity of the style proves the authorship of the work.
